text: heavy weight within dragging her insides down. Lara had caused this rift between them. She’d driven him away, when he had tried so hard to please her . “I…” He fell silent . Lara’s heart pounded, and she couldn’t get enough air in her lungs . “I don’t want to leave with this between us .” Everything stopped. His words hung in the air, and Lara couldn’t think. She stared at him, and he turned fully toward her . “The fault is mine. I thought your needs would be simple. That mine would be, too.” He shook his head, smoother this time than the last. “I’m learning. Forgive me, Lara Brooks .” Placing a hand on the counter, Lara pulled herself to her feet. Her fear was gone as quickly as it had come. Awe took its place. Learning. He was learning . “Only if you can forgive me, too,” she said, taking a hesitant step closer to him . “We both assumed understanding of one another, without considering what that really means.” He tilted his head down and ran a finger over the deep crack in the stone countertop. “I guess we really are made in the images of the Creators, if we share some of the same flaws .” Lara moved around the counter. “My experiences with bots don’t put them in a good light for me, Ronin. But the others I’ve dealt with wouldn’t have thought twice about doing that—” she pointed to the crack “— to me .” She stopped in front of him, looking up at his face. “And no bot’s ever apologized to me. For anything .” He shook his head, but his eyes ran over her body, engulfing her in their depth. “You’ve known so few of us. The ones you have as an example are…an anomaly .” “They were my reality. All the same…or worse .” “What can I do, Lara? To make you happy tonight? To see you smile before I leave ?” His words caught her by surprise, and, ridiculous as it felt, brought a smile to her lips. Tears burned in her eyes. She released a small, embarrassed laugh . “You’re turning me into such a sap,” she said, wiping her eyes with the back of her hand . He wanted her happy. Not just comfortable, not simply in good health to dance for him, but happy . No one besides Tabitha had ever cared about Lara’s feelings. No one until Ronin . He closed the short distance between them. Holding her gaze with his, he slowly raised a hand to her face and brushed the moisture from her cheek. His touch was so light, so gentle, that she couldn’t believe it was the same hand that shattered the counter . Lara stood totally still. The contact was foreign to her, yet it was…right. She understood that he’d stop if she asked, that she could pull away and he wouldn’t pursue her. Instead, she leaned into his touch, pressing her cheek to his palm. It was warm . Real . “I’ll come back as quickly as I can,” he said . “I’ll be waiting for you .” Chapter Fourteen N othing appeared out of the ordinary when Ronin stepped out of his dwelling. It was another typical day in Cheyenne — the sky was a yellow-tinged gray, the wind blew steady at about ten miles per hour, and the temperature had already hit seventy-nine degrees. It would be in the mid-eighties by the afternoon. The park across the street was empty; quiet and green, alive but unchanging . The sound of the deadbolt sliding into place as he locked it was oddly pronounced, shattering the morning’s monotony . Her words from a week ago echoed from his memory . I’m sure that’ll do wonders to keep bots out . He pushed himself forward, through the gates and into the Market, and then south into the human slums. All the while, his sensors searched for something, anything , abnormal enough to justify his turning around. He noted the discrepancy in his stride — seventy-five percent of his normal pace — but he couldn’t bring himself to alter it . Countless calculations and simulations said that she’d be fine during his absence. The chances of anything happening were slim, so long as she remained indoors. She knew to hide in the attic, if any bots came knocking . The words from the journal skittered across his processors, and he abruptly shifted functions. Diagnostics checks, system analysis, rough mapping of his potential route; anything but revisiting what he had read . Ronin forced his attention to his surroundings. The humans’ homes were constructed from the rubble Warlord didn’t want — they were collections of sometimes conflicting materials, often in poor condition, that should never have held together. Yet, somehow, they stood in defiance of the pristine buildings within the wall, in defiance of the Dust and its furious storms . There was no elegance here, no precision or cleanliness. Just rugged determination. They’d taken what the bots had deemed trash and repurposed it with imagination and ingenuity . Some of the residents stopped to stare at him as he passed. They would know, if only by the state of his clothing, that he wasn’t one of them. No one spoke to him . Many of the humans kept small gardens of stubby, struggling crops — some in patches of dirt behind their shacks, others in cracked pottery, some in ancient tubs and sinks. A few residences had makeshift pens with pigs, goats, or chickens. It was a far cry from the fields outside of Cheyenne — Warlord kept the agricultural bots working, and traded their harvests to the humans — but it was something . It was surviving . These people didn’t need programming to dictate their day-to-day activities. They did whatever their situation called for, whether it was growing food, repairing a building, or battling an enemy .
